title: Tale-Bearing and Spreading Mischief (Nameemah)
subtitle: The Walker Between People Who Plants Seeds of Hatred

verses.translation: And do not obey every worthless habitual swearer.
[And] a slanderer, going about with malicious gossip.

verses.arabic: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِن جَاءَكُمْ فَاسِقٌ بِنَبَإٍ فَتَبَيَّنُوا أَن تُصِيبُوا قَوْمًا بِجَهَالَةٍ فَتُصْبِحُوا عَلَىٰ مَا فَعَلْتُمْ نَادِمِينَ
verses.translation: O you who have believed, if there comes to you a disobedient one with information, investigate, lest you harm a people out of ignorance and become, over what you have done, regretful.

brief_explanation: The Quran describes the tale-bearer (namaam) as a lowly, contemptible figure — 'mashsha'in bi-namim' (a walker about with malicious gossip) — pairing this description with habitual oath-breaking and moral baseness. Nameemah is the act of carrying words between people with the intent to damage relationships and sow discord, and the Prophet (peace be upon him) declared that 'the namam will not enter Paradise.' The Quran warns believers not to act on information brought by a fasiq without verification — acknowledging that the tale-bearer injects poison into communities through unverified reports that destroy friendships, marriages, and alliances. This sin's primary evil is not mere lying but the deliberate cultivation of enmity between believers.
capital_punishment: There is no hadd (fixed corporal punishment) for nameemah. It is a major sin of speech addressed through sincere repentance, ceasing the behaviour, and — where possible — undoing the harm caused by making peace between those who were turned against one another. The state may impose ta'zir if nameemah is proven to have caused measurable harm to individuals or to social cohesion. If the tale-bearing involved false information that constitutes slander (qadhf), the hadd of 80 lashes would apply under those specific conditions.
